title: Greek Yogurt Bagels
summary: Easy 2-ingredient style bagels with Greek yogurt and flour, great as a high-protein breakfast base.
ingredients: 2 cups self-rising flour (or flour + baking powder + salt)
Everything bagel seasoning or sesame seeds
Optional: pinch of salt if using homemade self-rising mix
steps: Preheat oven to 375°F.
Mix flour and Greek yogurt until a shaggy dough forms.
Knead lightly until smooth enough to handle.
Divide into 4 to 6 portions and shape into bagels.
Brush with egg wash and top with seasoning.
Bake until golden and cooked through, about 22 to 28 minutes.
Cool slightly before slicing.
notes: Good with egg sandwiches, smoked salmon, or breakfast sausage. Texture lands between a chewy roll and a classic bagel.

title: High-Protein Pizza Dough
summary: Greek yogurt and bone broth powder boosted dough for thinner homemade pizzas or calzones.
steps: Mix dry ingredients together.
Fold in Greek yogurt and combine into a dough.
Knead lightly until mostly smooth.
Rest 10 to 15 minutes.
Roll thin for pizza or divide for calzones.
Par-bake if desired, then top and finish baking.
notes: Great base for calzones or high-protein pizzas with turkey, chicken, or light cheese blends.

title: Turkey Ragu
summary: Long-simmered spicy turkey ragu with onions, garlic, tomato paste, crushed tomatoes, herbs, and parmesan rind.
steps: Brown turkey and season lightly, then remove.
Cook onion in olive oil until softened and lightly golden.
Add garlic and tomato paste; cook until darkened slightly.
Add crushed tomatoes, herbs, chili flakes, paprika, and parmesan rind.
Return turkey to the pot and simmer low for at least 45 to 90 minutes.
Adjust seasoning and reduce until rich and concentrated.
notes: Base recipe that can feed pasta, lasagna soup, baked pasta, or calzones. One of the core Water St utility sauces.

title: Lasagna Soup
summary: Soup version of lasagna built off the turkey ragu base, finished with broken noodles and cheesy topping.
steps: Bring turkey ragu up with broth to soup consistency.
Add broken lasagna noodles and simmer until tender.
Mix cottage cheese or ricotta with parmesan and pepper.
Ladle soup into bowls and top with cheese mixture and mozzarella.
Optional: broil individual bowls briefly or stir cheese in.
notes: Very easy way to turn leftover ragu into a different meal without feeling repetitive.

title: Red Salsa
summary: Chile-forward salsa with dried chiles, garlic, onion, and a deeper roasted flavor.
steps: Toast chiles lightly until fragrant, being careful not to burn them.
Soften chiles in hot water.
Char onion, garlic, and tomatoes if using.
Blend everything until smooth.
Season with salt and thin to desired texture.
Optional: simmer briefly to deepen flavor.
notes: Works for tacos, eggs, queso fundido, grilled meats, or breakfast bowls.

title: Espagueti Verde
summary: Creamy poblano green spaghetti with onion, garlic, crema-style body, and roasted pepper flavor.
ingredients: Mexican crema or Greek yogurt / cottage cheese swap
steps: Roast poblanos until blistered; steam and peel.
Cook onion and garlic until softened.
Blend poblanos with onion, garlic, crema or yogurt blend, and a splash of milk/broth.
Cook spaghetti until al dente.
Simmer sauce briefly, then toss with pasta.
Season and serve.
notes: Good as-is or with grilled chicken added for more protein.

title: Spicy Marinara / Pizza Sauce
summary: Fast red sauce for pizza, calzones, pasta, or dipping, with garlic, tomato paste, and chili heat.
steps: Cook garlic gently in olive oil.
Stir in tomato paste and cook 1 to 2 minutes.
Add tomatoes, oregano, basil, chili flakes, and salt.
Simmer until slightly thickened.
Blend if smoother texture is desired.
notes: Keeps the flavor profile sharp and clean for pizzas and calzones without getting watery.

title: Homemade Alfredo
summary: Flexible Alfredo with cottage cheese or evaporated milk versions, built to stay creamy without being absurdly heavy.
ingredients: Cottage cheese and milk OR evaporated milk
steps: Blend cottage cheese with milk if using that version.
Warm butter and garlic gently.
Add dairy base and heat carefully without breaking.
Whisk in parmesan and black pepper.
Use pasta water to loosen until glossy.
Toss with hot pasta immediately.
notes: You can lean richer or higher-protein depending on the dairy base. Great jumping-off point for chipotle or calabrian variants.

title: Zucchini Bread
summary: Moist quick bread that works as a lower-guilt breakfast bake or snack.
steps: Preheat oven to 350°F and grease loaf pan.
Mix wet ingredients together.
Fold in zucchini.
Whisk dry ingredients separately, then combine gently.
Bake until set and a tester comes out mostly clean.
Cool before slicing.
notes: Can be adjusted with walnuts or chocolate chips, but the simple version is already solid.

title: Calzones
summary: Folded pizza pockets using your protein pizza dough, filled with marinara, mozzarella, and meat or veg.
steps: Divide and roll dough into circles.
Add filling to one half, keeping edges clear.
Fold over and crimp shut.
Brush with egg wash if desired.
Bake at 425°F until browned and cooked through.
Rest a few minutes before cutting.
notes: Great use for leftover turkey ragu or cheese-heavy fillings without making a full pizza.

title: Spaghetti alla Carbonara
summary: Classic-ish carbonara approach with eggs, cheese, black pepper, and rendered meat fat for sauce body.
ingredients: Guanciale, pancetta, bacon, or a substitute meat
steps: Cook pasta in salted water.
Render meat until crisp and reserve some fat.
Whisk eggs, cheese, and black pepper.
Toss hot pasta with meat and a little fat off heat.
Temper with pasta water and add egg mixture while stirring constantly.
Adjust with more pasta water until glossy.
notes: Needs heat control. Too hot and it scrambles, too cool and it clumps.

title: Turkey Breakfast Sausages
summary: Homemade breakfast sausage patties using lean turkey and a breakfast-spice profile.
steps: Mix turkey with seasonings gently.
Rest mixture briefly if time allows.
Form into small patties.
Cook in skillet until browned and fully cooked through.
Serve with eggs, tacos, or bagels.
notes: Easy meal prep protein that feels better than most store-bought turkey breakfast sausage.

title: Garlic-Herb Flatbreads
summary: Soft skillet or oven flatbreads with garlic and herb flavor, useful for wraps, dips, or quick pizza bases.
ingredients: Greek yogurt or water depending on version
steps: Mix dough ingredients and knead briefly.
Rest 10 to 20 minutes.
Divide and roll thin.
Cook in hot skillet or bake until puffed and spotted.
Brush with garlic-herb butter or oil.
notes: Can sit between naan, pita, and yogurt flatbread depending on hydration.

title: High-Protein Calabrian Chicken Pasta
summary: Creamy spicy chicken pasta with Calabrian chili heat and a lighter protein-forward sauce base.
ingredients: Cottage cheese or Greek yogurt sauce base
steps: Season and cook chicken, then slice or chop.
Cook pasta to al dente.
Build sauce with garlic, chili paste, and blended dairy base.
Add parmesan and thin with pasta water.
Toss in pasta and chicken until coated.
Adjust heat, salt, and texture.
notes: A good weeknight bridge between vodka sauce energy and Alfredo structure, but higher protein.

title: Queso Fundido
summary: Melty cheese skillet with poblano and optional turkey chorizo or sausage for extra protein.
ingredients: Turkey sausage or turkey chorizo optional
steps: Roast or char poblano, then peel and slice.
Cook onion and sausage if using.
Add peppers back in and top with cheese.
Bake or cover in skillet until melted and bubbling.
Serve hot with tortillas, chips, or flatbread.
notes: Peak party food. Also works as a protein-heavy late meal if you keep the sausage in.

title: Breakfast Tacos
summary: Turkey sausage, eggs, cheese, and salsa breakfast tacos that actually hold up for repeat mornings.
steps: Cook sausage and set aside.
Scramble eggs softly.
Warm tortillas.
Fill with eggs, sausage, cheese, and salsa.
Serve immediately.
notes: Simple, reliable, and easy to macro-manage depending on tortilla and cheese choices.

title: Butter Chicken (WIP)
summary: Yogurt-marinated chicken in a spiced tomato-cream sauce. Good but not yet dialed in — currently runs too acidic. Next attempt: cashew paste and a pinch of sugar to balance. The marinade is solid; the sauce needs refinement.
ingredients: 1.5 lbs chicken breast, cubed and scored
1/2 cup whole milk yogurt (or 0% skyr + 1 tbsp heavy cream)
4 cloves garlic, minced (or ginger garlic paste)
1.5 tsp Kashmiri chili powder (or paprika + pinch cayenne)
1 can crushed tomatoes (14oz) — San Marzano preferred
Pinch of sugar (next time — helps acidity)
Cashew paste — 1/4 cup soaked raw cashews blended smooth (next time — key for creaminess)
1 scoop Ancient Nutrition bone broth powder
steps: Marinate chicken overnight — combine all marinade ingredients, coat chicken thoroughly, refrigerate.
Cook marinated chicken in a screaming hot cast iron or under broiler until charred in spots. Set aside. Do not skip the char.
In a wide pan, cook onion in 1 tbsp butter + oil over medium until deeply golden, 12-15 min minimum.
Add garlic, ginger, cook 2 min.
Add tomato paste, cook until darkened, 2 min.
Add dry spices, toast 60 seconds.
Add crushed tomatoes and pinch of sugar. Simmer 20-25 min until thickened and oil begins to separate.
Blend sauce smooth with immersion blender.
Stir in blended cottage cheese and cashew paste.
Add charred chicken, simmer together 10-15 min.
Stir in heavy cream and bone broth powder, simmer gently 5 min.
Finish with cold butter off heat and kasuri methi.
Adjust salt and serve.
notes: WORK IN PROGRESS. Current issue: sauce runs too acidic/tomatoey. Fixes to try next time: (1) cashew paste — soak 1/4 cup raw cashews, blend smooth, add before cream; (2) pinch of sugar during tomato simmer; (3) San Marzano tomatoes instead of regular crushed; (4) longer tomato simmer before blending. The marinade is solid and does not need changes. The char step is non-negotiable. Milk and cream do not fix acidity — they only thin the sauce.

title: Chipotle Feta Tuna Peppers
summary: Charred red bell peppers stuffed with spiced tuna and broiled with Italian cheese. Two proven variants: the original feta + fire sauce version, and a taco-seasoned version with toom and chipotle aioli. Both fast, high-protein, and easy to improvise from pantry staples.
ingredients: 1 can Safe Catch yellowfin tuna (4.5oz) — strongly preferred over Kirkland albacore for flavor
3 Taco Bell fire sauce packets (or equivalent hot sauce)
1 red bell pepper, halved and deseeded
Dried scallions and parsley for finishing
Next time: chipotle chili powder + squeeze of lime in the mixture
1-2 cans Kirkland albacore white tuna (works fine cooked and seasoned, just more pungent raw)
1 red bell pepper, halved and deseeded
steps: Halve and deseed the red bell pepper. Spray or drizzle oil on the skin side.
Char the skin side directly over a burner, under a broiler, or in a toaster oven.
Place charred peppers in a bag or covered bowl to steam for 5-10 minutes. Peel off the skin.
--- ORIGINAL VERSION ---
Mix tuna with chipotle mayo, fire sauce, and feta until combined.
--- TACO VARIANT ---
Cook tuna in a pan with a drizzle of olive oil and taco seasoning. Add a splash of beef broth to loosen and give it a taco bowl vibe. Cook until fragrant and slightly reduced.
Off heat, stir in chipotle aioli and toom until fully combined.
--- BOTH VERSIONS ---
Stuff pepper halves generously with tuna mixture.
Top with a pinch of shredded Italian cheese.
Broil until cheese bubbles and starts to brown.
Original: finish with dried scallions, parsley, and smoked flaky salt. Taco variant: finish with Mateo's salsa.
notes: Safe Catch yellowfin (39g protein, ~165 cal per 4.5oz can) is clearly the better eating tuna — no fishy smell, could eat it straight from the can. Kirkland albacore (42g protein, ~180 cal per can) is more pungent raw but totally fine once cooked and seasoned. Use Safe Catch when you want to enjoy it; Kirkland when it's what you have. The charred-and-steamed pepper step is the same for both versions — don't skip it. Toom is the move in the taco variant: the garlic richness bridges the chipotle and seasoning perfectly.

title: Sushi Bake Tuna Handrolls
summary: Sushi bake-inspired tuna cooked down with sesame, sriracha, tamari, and tomato, finished with a crispy panko crust and served as seaweed handrolls with chili crunch.
ingredients: 1 can diced tomatoes (Red Gold or similar)
Crunchy garlic chili oil for dipping
steps: Cook tuna in a pan with sesame oil, sriracha, and tamari over medium heat.
Add the entire can of diced tomatoes and stir to combine.
Cook down until most liquid has reduced and mixture is thick.
Transfer to a small square sheet tray or oven-safe dish.
Top evenly with panko, sesame seeds, and dots of kewpie mayo.
Bake at 400°F for approximately 15 minutes until top is golden and crispy.
Cut into squares.
Wrap each square in a sheet of dried seaweed to form a handroll.
Dip in crunchy garlic chili oil and eat immediately.
notes: Originally created while moving out of an apartment using only what was left in the pantry. The diced tomato is the unexpected unlock — it adds acid and sweetness that completely neutralizes the fishiness. The panko crust is non-negotiable for texture contrast.

title: Mexican Cottage Pie
summary: A Mexican-inspired twist on the British classic — birria-spiced ground beef under a jalapeño cheddar mash. Baked in cast iron until the peaks go crispy.
ingredients: 1 Carroll Shelby's Birria Taco Seasoning packet
2 lb russet or Yukon gold potatoes
1-2 tbsp chipotle mayo (stir into mash — highly recommended)
steps: Peel and cube potatoes. Add to cold salted water, bring to boil, cook until fork tender.
Heat olive oil in a large oven-safe skillet over medium-high. Cook onion and garlic until softened, about 3 min.
Add ground beef, break up, and brown fully. Season with salt and pepper.
Add tomato paste and cook into the meat for 1 minute.
Add birria seasoning packet, Worcestershire, and beef stock. Simmer until liquid reduces into a thick gravy. Taste and adjust salt.
Drain potatoes and mash well. Add cream, olive oil, chipotle mayo, and mash until smooth.
Fold in both cheeses and pickled jalapeños. Taste — it should be well seasoned and a little spicy.
Spoon mash over the beef filling and spread to the edges. Fork up the surface to create peaks.
Bake at 400°F until the top is golden and crispy in spots.
Rest 5 minutes before serving.
notes: The birria packet does most of the seasoning heavy lifting — taste the filling before adding extra salt. The chipotle mayo in the mash is not in the original recipe but is the move — adds smokiness and richness that ties the whole thing together. Serve with Lime Sesame Toom Vinaigrette over baby spinach to cut through the richness. Leftovers reheat very well.

title: Lime Sesame Toom Vinaigrette
summary: A garlicky, acidic dressing that cuts through rich, heavy dishes. Built around toom and lime with a touch of sesame. Takes 2 minutes and punches way above its weight.
ingredients: 1.5 tbsp fresh lime juice (about half a lime)
steps: Combine lime juice, apple cider vinegar, sesame oil, and toom in a small bowl.
Whisk until the toom emulsifies into the dressing.
Add honey or sugar to balance the acid.
Taste before adding salt — toom is already salty.
Dress baby spinach or greens lightly right before serving so they don't wilt.
Toss and serve immediately.
notes: Go easy on the sesame oil — a little goes a very long way. Pairs especially well with the Mexican Cottage Pie as a counter to the richness of the mash.

title: Skyr Chipotle Crema
summary: A high-protein sour cream substitute built on 0% skyr. Tangy, creamy, slightly smoky, and takes 2 minutes. Use anywhere you'd reach for sour cream or crema — tacos, taco bowls, cottage pie, eggs.
steps: Combine skyr, Duke's mayo, chipotle mayo, and heavy cream in a small bowl.
Stir until fully smooth and combined.
Taste and adjust salt.
Use immediately or refrigerate up to 4 days.
notes: The Duke's mayo is what gives it body and that slight richness that plain Greek yogurt lacks. Chipotle mayo adds smokiness without heat. Works as a 1:1 swap for sour cream in basically any application.

title: Potato Wedge Taco Bowl
summary: Seasoned ground beef and crispy air fryer potato wedges served bowl-style with fresh avocado, skyr chipotle crema, and Mexican cheeses.
ingredients: 1 lb 93/7 ground beef (or 85/15 if that's what you have)
1.5 lbs baby potatoes or fingerlings, halved
Fire sauce or hot sauce to finish
steps: Toss halved potatoes with olive oil, smoked paprika, garlic powder, chili powder, oregano, and salt.
Air fry at 400°F for 18-22 minutes, shaking halfway, until crispy and golden. Can be made ahead and reheated.
Heat avocado oil in a skillet over medium-high. Cook onion and garlic until golden, about 4 minutes.
Add ground beef, break up, and cook through.
Add taco seasoning and a splash of beef bone broth. Stir and simmer until liquid cooks down.
If using pre-made potato wedges, return them to the same pan after removing the beef. Deglaze with a splash of bone broth to pick up the beef fond and toss the potatoes until coated.
Add beef and potatoes to a large bowl. Fan sliced avocado over one side. Spoon skyr chipotle crema alongside.
Top with cotija, Mexican cheese blend, and torn cilantro. Finish potatoes with fire sauce.
notes: The reheat trick — deglazing the beef pan with bone broth and tossing the potatoes in the fond — is what makes the potatoes exceptional. Don't skip it if you're using pre-made wedges. Baby potato medley or fingerlings hold their shape best; gold potatoes work but can be softer.

title: Guanciale Carbonara with Fresh Fettuccine
summary: Classic Roman carbonara made with guanciale, fresh fettuccine, extra egg yolks, and a Parmesan-Grana Padano blend. No cream, no shortcuts. The sauce is pure emulsified eggs, pasta water, and rendered pork fat.
ingredients: Small drizzle olive oil if dough is dry
20g Grana Padano, finely grated (plus more to finish)
Freshly cracked black pepper — a lot, more than you think
steps: Mix flour with 3 whole eggs into a dough. Add a small drizzle of olive oil if dry. Knead until smooth, wrap, rest at least 30 minutes.

Divide rested dough into 3-4 portions, keep others covered. Laminate at setting 1 (fold in thirds, rotate, repeat 2-3 times). Roll down through settings 2, 3, 4, 5. Stop at 5-6 — you want body, not paper thin.
Flour both sides generously before cutting. Feed through fettuccine attachment, toss in more flour immediately, nest into portions. Rest 10-15 minutes before cooking.
Whisk 2 whole eggs, 3 yolks, Parmesan, and Grana Padano together until fully combined. Add black pepper generously. Set aside at room temperature.
Add guanciale to a cold pan and bring to medium-low heat. Render slowly until fat is translucent and edges are crispy, about 8-10 minutes. Do not rush. Turn off heat.

Bring a large pot of lightly salted water to a boil. Go lighter on salt than you normally would — guanciale and cheese both carry significant salt. Drop fettuccine and cook until just barely al dente, about 2 minutes. Reserve 120ml pasta water before draining.
Using tongs pull pasta directly into the guanciale pan. Toss in the guanciale fat over low heat for 30 seconds to coat every strand.
Remove pan from heat entirely. Wait 60 seconds — critical step. If pan is too hot the eggs scramble.
Pour egg mixture over pasta and toss constantly and vigorously. Add pasta water a splash at a time to loosen and emulsify. Keep tossing until glossy and creamy, coating every strand.
Plate immediately. Finish with extra Grana Padano, more cracked black pepper, and fresh parsley if using. Eat right away.
notes: The guanciale is non-negotiable — pancetta is a consolation prize. The pan cooling step before eggs is the most critical technique in this dish. Salt the pasta water lighter than usual since every other component is already salty. Pasta water is your emulsification tool — keep adding until the sauce is glossy and loose, it tightens as it sits. No cream ever. First attempt came out restaurant quality — fix next time: lighter hand on pasta water salt. The Parmesan + Grana Padano blend is excellent — nuttier and more complex than Parmesan alone.

title: Beef and Broccoli Stir Fry
summary: HEB grass-fed stir fry strips with a tamari-baking soda overnight marinade and a savory Worcestershire-based sauce. Velvety restaurant-style texture from the baking soda tenderize. Finished with scallions over white rice.
ingredients: 1.21 lbs HEB grass-fed beef stir fry strips
2 tbsp Worcestershire sauce (oyster sauce sub — use 3 tbsp actual oyster sauce if you have it)
steps: Combine all marinade ingredients, toss with beef, cover and refrigerate overnight. Baking soda is the key to velvety texture — do not skip. Max 24 hours or texture goes mealy. If extending past 24 hours, rinse off marinade and refrigerate plain.
Whisk all sauce ingredients together before anything hits the pan. Have it ready to go.
Blanch broccoli florets in boiling water for 90 seconds, then transfer immediately to an ice bath. Drain and set aside. Keeps it bright green and slightly crisp.
Pull beef from marinade and pat completely dry — twice if needed. Moisture kills the sear.

Heat avocado oil in a wok or large stainless pan over screaming high heat until just starting to smoke. Sear beef in a single layer, 60 to 90 seconds per side. Work in batches — don't crowd the pan. Remove and set aside.
Drop to medium-high. Add garlic and ginger, stir 30 seconds until fragrant.
Pour in sauce mixture and stir — it thickens fast. Add broccoli and toss to coat.
Return beef to pan, toss everything 60 seconds. Serve over rice with sliced scallions on top.
notes: Grass-fed beef is leaner than conventional and cooks faster — it can get chewy if overdone. Err on the side of undercooking during the sear since it carries over in the sauce. Worcestershire is a solid oyster sauce sub but stronger — 2 tbsp is the ceiling. Add Worcestershire to the sauce only, never the marinade. The baking soda marinade is the single biggest unlock for that velvety Chinese restaurant texture. If the broth got into the marinade by accident (it happens), just make sure you pat the beef extra dry before searing.

title: White Bean Ragù Soup
summary: A thick, hearty bean-and-ragù soup that turns leftover ragù into a brand new meal. Cannellini beans add body and a massive fiber boost, beef bone broth deepens it, and a splash of milk tempers the tomato acidity. One of the most macro-efficient meals in the rotation — high protein, high fiber, genuinely satiating. Holds beautifully in the fridge.
ingredients: ~625g leftover Turkey Ragù (best with the doubled mixed-meat version: 1lb 93/7 ground turkey + 1lb 90/10 ground beef, all other ragù ingredients doubled — heavier, beefier profile)
425g cannellini (white kidney) beans — full 15oz can, drained and rinsed; mash ~half a cup with a fork for body, leave the rest whole
~315g beef bone broth (Kettle & Fire or similar)
Various herbs and seasonings to taste
Optional: fresh lasagna sheets, torn into pieces, for extra carbs/texture
Optional: dollop of ricotta on top for richness
steps: Add ~2 tsp olive oil to the pot and warm over medium.
Add the leftover ragù and warm through.
Pour in the beef bone broth, bring to a simmer, and let it marry 15-20 min.
Stir in the mashed beans early for body, then add the whole beans near the end to warm through.
If using lasagna sheets, tear them into pieces and simmer in the soup until tender, a few minutes before serving.
Finish with a splash of Mootopia milk to add creaminess and temper the tomato acidity.
Adjust herbs and seasoning at the close — taste first, since both the bone broth and the pre-seasoned ragù bring salt.
Top each bowl with shaved parmesan, and a dollop of ricotta if using.
notes: Built from leftover ragù, so the exact macros shift batch to batch — the numbers here are for the doubled mixed-meat version, beans, broth, oil and parm only (no pasta/ricotta). The beans are the unlock: ~26g of fiber from a single can turns this into a fiber bomb without changing the flavor much. The mashed-then-whole bean technique gives you creamy body plus intact beans for texture. A splash of milk at the end is the move for cutting the tomato acidity. Torn fresh lasagna sheets turn it into more of a lasagna soup if you want the carbs; a dollop of ricotta adds richness and texture. Scales and reheats perfectly — a full pot can carry an entire day's eating. Whole pot (no pasta/ricotta) runs ~1,489 cal / ~112g protein / ~32g fiber.

title: Saag Chicken
summary: A high-protein take on saag built in one Dutch oven. Marinated chicken gets browned, then the fond becomes the base for onions, ginger, garlic and Indian spices. A full pound of spinach wilts down with broth, then blended cottage cheese gets stick-blended right into the greens for a creamy, protein-loaded sauce — no heavy cream base needed. Holds for days and the macros are excellent.
ingredients: ~1.5 lbs chicken (breast or thigh), marinated (yogurt or skyr + ginger garlic paste + Indian spices)
1 tbsp fresh ginger, grated (or ginger garlic paste)
Indian spices to taste (garam masala, cumin, coriander, turmeric, Kashmiri chili powder)
Kasuri methi (dried fenugreek), crushed, to finish
Optional: 1 scoop bone broth powder for extra protein
Serve with rice, naan, or paratha
steps: Brown the marinated chicken in a Dutch oven until it has good color and char in spots. Remove and set aside.
In the fond left behind, cook the onions with a little salt until softened and golden.
Add ginger, garlic, and Indian spices; cook until fragrant, ~2 min.
Add the full pound of spinach and the chicken broth. Close the lid and let it steam and wilt down.
Add the blended cottage cheese (and bone broth powder if using). Use a stick blender to blend everything together and crush the spinach into a smooth saag.
Add the browned chicken back in. Salt to taste.
Finish with a drizzle of cream (~1 tsp) and crushed kasuri methi on top.
Serve over rice, with naan, or with paratha.
notes: The cottage cheese blended directly into the wilted spinach is the move — it adds body and a big protein boost without the calories of a cream-heavy saag. The fond from browning the chicken is where a lot of the flavor lives, so build the onion base right in it. Full pot runs roughly ~805-850 cal / ~125-135g protein (sauce + chicken, before rice/naan). Makes 3 servings and holds well for 2-3 days. A scoop of bone broth powder pushes the protein even higher. Don't skip the kasuri methi finish — it's what makes it taste authentically saag.

title: Birria Chili
summary: A weeknight chili built on birria-spiced beef - because birria seasoning (guajillo, ancho, arbol, cumin, Mexican oregano, garlic, a vinegar tang) is already the chili flavor family. Deep, beefy, mildly spicy, protein-dense. Great as tacos and bowls, or Cincinnati-style over protein spaghetti.
ingredients: 1 lb prepared beef birria (e.g. Trader Joe's), shredded
1 can (28oz) Rosa or San Marzano crushed tomatoes (lower acid, slightly sweet)
Smoked paprika and/or chipotle to taste (season lightly - the beef already carries chile)
Cumin and Mexican oregano to taste
Pinch of sugar (balances the birria vinegar + tomato acidity)
Optional: 1 can pinto or black beans
To finish: lime, raw diced onion, cilantro
steps: Warm olive oil in a pot and cook the onion until soft and lightly golden. Add garlic and cook 1 min.
Stir in tomato paste and cook until darkened, about 2 min.
Add the birria beef (and optional ground beef) and stir to combine and heat through.

Add crushed tomatoes and a splash of bone broth. Season LIGHTLY - taste first, the beef already carries chile, cumin, and oregano - then nudge with smoked paprika or chipotle. Add a pinch of sugar to balance the acid.
Simmer low 30-45 min until rich and slightly thickened. If it tastes sharp, simmer longer or add another pinch of sugar (same fix as the butter chicken).
Stir in beans if using and warm through.
Finish with lime, raw diced onion, and cilantro.
notes: The birria beef is a head start, not a complication - it is the chili flavor family already. Two adjustments because of it: season lighter on top (the beef carries chile/cumin/oregano), and mind the acid (Rosa San Marzano crushed plus a pinch of sugar). Goes Cincinnati-style over Brami protein spaghetti with cheddar and raw onion - that is the chili pasta. Or as tacos/bowl with Mission fiber tortillas. Macros are approximate, estimated from logged portions (a 418g plain bowl ran ~533 cal / ~51g protein / ~8g fiber).

title: Creamed Spinach Saag Pasta
summary: Italian-Indian-Southern fusion: Brami protein spaghetti folded into a creamed-spinach saag-style sauce built on a full pound of spinach, a whole head of garlic, cottage cheese, parmesan, and kasoori methi. Creamed-spinach richness meets saag spice on a high-fiber lupini base.
ingredients: 455g cooked Brami protein spaghetti (~207g dry equiv)
1/4 cup Mootopia (or other high-protein) cottage cheese, blended smooth
15g shredded parmesan (10g mixed in + 5g to finish)
Italian + Indian spices to taste (Italian seasoning/oregano + cumin/garam masala/chili)
Kasoori methi (dried fenugreek), crushed, to finish
steps: Cook onions in the oil and butter over medium until soft and lightly golden.
Add garlic and ginger garlic paste; cook 1-2 min until fragrant.
Add spinach in batches, wilting it down. Season with the Italian and Indian spices.
Add broth and simmer briefly to meld. Blend smooth with an immersion blender for a saag-style sauce, or leave rustic.
Off high heat, stir in the blended cottage cheese and 10g parmesan so it stays creamy and doesn't split.
Fold in the cooked Brami and toss to coat, loosening with a splash more broth if needed.
Finish with crushed kasoori methi and the remaining 5g parmesan on the plate.
notes: Fusion of creamed spinach, Indian saag, and Italian pasta. Full pot ~1,700 cal / ~83g protein / ~34g fiber - Brami and the full pound of spinach drive the fiber. Vegetarian as written; folds in shredded chicken beautifully for a protein bump. Kasoori methi is what pushes it from creamed-spinach pasta toward saag - don't skip it. Blend the cottage cheese smooth before adding so it melts like cream instead of going curdy.
